units.text: Enter a company of mutinous Citizens, with staves, clubs, and other weapons

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: Before we proceed any further, hear me speak.

units.speaker: All
lines: Speak, speak.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: You are all resolved rather to die than to famish?

units.speaker: All
lines: Resolved. resolved.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: First, you know Caius Marcius is chief enemy to the people.

units.speaker: All
lines: We know't, we know't.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: Let us kill him, and we'll have corn at our own price.
Is't a verdict?

units.speaker: All
lines: No more talking on't; let it be done: away, away!

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: One word, good citizens.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: We are accounted poor citizens, the patricians good.
What authority surfeits on would relieve us: if they
would yield us but the superfluity, while it were
wholesome, we might guess they relieved us humanely;
but they think we are too dear: the leanness that
afflicts us, the object of our misery, is as an
inventory to particularise their abundance; our
sufferance is a gain to them Let us revenge this with
our pikes, ere we become rakes: for the gods know I
speak this in hunger for bread, not in thirst for revenge.

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: Would you proceed especially against Caius Marcius?

units.speaker: All
lines: Against him first: he's a very dog to the commonalty.

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: Consider you what services he has done for his country?

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: Very well; and could be content to give him good
report fort, but that he pays himself with being proud.

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: Nay, but speak not maliciously.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: I say unto you, what he hath done famously, he did
it to that end: though soft-conscienced men can be
content to say it was for his country he did it to
please his mother and to be partly proud; which he
is, even till the altitude of his virtue.

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: What he cannot help in his nature, you account a
vice in him. You must in no way say he is covetous.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: If I must not, I need not be barren of accusations;
he hath faults, with surplus, to tire in repetition.
What shouts are these? The other side o' the city
is risen: why stay we prating here? to the Capitol!

units.speaker: All
lines: Come, come.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: Soft! who comes here?

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: Worthy Menenius Agrippa; one that hath always loved
the people.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: He's one honest enough: would all the rest were so!

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: What work's, my countrymen, in hand? where go you
With bats and clubs? The matter? speak, I pray you.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: Our business is not unknown to the senate; they have
had inkling this fortnight what we intend to do,
which now we'll show 'em in deeds. They say poor
suitors have strong breaths: they shall know we
have strong arms too.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Why, masters, my good friends, mine honest neighbours,
Will you undo yourselves?

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: We cannot, sir, we are undone already.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: I tell you, friends, most charitable care
Have the patricians of you. For your wants,
Your suffering in this dearth, you may as well
Strike at the heaven with your staves as lift them
Against the Roman state, whose course will on
The way it takes, cracking ten thousand curbs
Of more strong link asunder than can ever
Appear in your impediment. For the dearth,
The gods, not the patricians, make it, and
Your knees to them, not arms, must help. Alack,
You are transported by calamity
Thither where more attends you, and you slander
The helms o' the state, who care for you like fathers,
When you curse them as enemies.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: Care for us! True, indeed! They ne'er cared for us
yet: suffer us to famish, and their store-houses
crammed with grain; make edicts for usury, to
support usurers; repeal daily any wholesome act
established against the rich, and provide more
piercing statutes daily, to chain up and restrain
the poor. If the wars eat us not up, they will; and
there's all the love they bear us.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Either you must
Confess yourselves wondrous malicious,
Or be accused of folly. I shall tell you
A pretty tale: it may be you have heard it;
But, since it serves my purpose, I will venture
To stale 't a little more.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: Well, I'll hear it, sir: yet you must not think to
fob off our disgrace with a tale: but, an 't please
you, deliver.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: There was a time when all the body's members
Rebell'd against the belly, thus accused it:
That only like a gulf it did remain
I' the midst o' the body, idle and unactive,
Still cupboarding the viand, never bearing
Like labour with the rest, where the other instruments
Did see and hear, devise, instruct, walk, feel,
And, mutually participate, did minister
Unto the appetite and affection common
Of the whole body. The belly answer'd--

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: Well, sir, what answer made the belly?

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Sir, I shall tell you. With a kind of smile,
Which ne'er came from the lungs, but even thus--
For, look you, I may make the belly smile
As well as speak--it tauntingly replied
To the discontented members, the mutinous parts
That envied his receipt; even so most fitly
As you malign our senators for that
They are not such as you.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: Your belly's answer? What!
The kingly-crowned head, the vigilant eye,
The counsellor heart, the arm our soldier,
Our steed the leg, the tongue our trumpeter.
With other muniments and petty helps
In this our fabric, if that they--

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: What then?
'Fore me, this fellow speaks! What then? what then?

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: Should by the cormorant belly be restrain'd,
Who is the sink o' the body,--

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Well, what then?

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: The former agents, if they did complain,
What could the belly answer?

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: I will tell you
If you'll bestow a small--of what you have little--
Patience awhile, you'll hear the belly's answer.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: Ye're long about it.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Note me this, good friend;
Your most grave belly was deliberate,
Not rash like his accusers, and thus answer'd:
'True is it, my incorporate friends,' quoth he,
'That I receive the general food at first,
Which you do live upon; and fit it is,
Because I am the store-house and the shop
Of the whole body: but, if you do remember,
I send it through the rivers of your blood,
Even to the court, the heart, to the seat o' the brain;
And, through the cranks and offices of man,
The strongest nerves and small inferior veins
From me receive that natural competency
Whereby they live: and though that all at once,
You, my good friends,'--this says the belly, mark me,--

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: Ay, sir; well, well.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: 'Though all at once cannot
See what I do deliver out to each,
Yet I can make my audit up, that all
From me do back receive the flour of all,
And leave me but the bran.' What say you to't?

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: It was an answer: how apply you this?

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: The senators of Rome are this good belly,
And you the mutinous members; for examine
Their counsels and their cares, digest things rightly
Touching the weal o' the common, you shall find
No public benefit which you receive
But it proceeds or comes from them to you
And no way from yourselves. What do you think,
You, the great toe of this assembly?

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: I the great toe! why the great toe?

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: For that, being one o' the lowest, basest, poorest,
Of this most wise rebellion, thou go'st foremost:
Thou rascal, that art worst in blood to run,
Lead'st first to win some vantage.
But make you ready your stiff bats and clubs:
Rome and her rats are at the point of battle;
The one side must have bale.
Hail, noble Marcius!

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Thanks. What's the matter, you dissentious rogues,
That, rubbing the poor itch of your opinion,
Make yourselves scabs?

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: We have ever your good word.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: He that will give good words to thee will flatter
Beneath abhorring. What would you have, you curs,
That like nor peace nor war? the one affrights you,
The other makes you proud. He that trusts to you,
Where he should find you lions, finds you hares;
Where foxes, geese: you are no surer, no,
Than is the coal of fire upon the ice,
Or hailstone in the sun. Your virtue is
To make him worthy whose offence subdues him
And curse that justice did it.
Who deserves greatness
Deserves your hate; and your affections are
A sick man's appetite, who desires most that
Which would increase his evil. He that depends
Upon your favours swims with fins of lead
And hews down oaks with rushes. Hang ye! Trust Ye?
With every minute you do change a mind,
And call him noble that was now your hate,
Him vile that was your garland. What's the matter,
That in these several places of the city
You cry against the noble senate, who,
Under the gods, keep you in awe, which else
Would feed on one another? What's their seeking?

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: For corn at their own rates; whereof, they say,
The city is well stored.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Hang 'em! They say!
They'll sit by the fire, and presume to know
What's done i' the Capitol; who's like to rise,
Who thrives and who declines; side factions
and give out
Conjectural marriages; making parties strong
And feebling such as stand not in their liking
Below their cobbled shoes. They say there's
grain enough!
Would the nobility lay aside their ruth,
And let me use my sword, I'll make a quarry
With thousands of these quarter'd slaves, as high
As I could pick my lance.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Nay, these are almost thoroughly persuaded;
For though abundantly they lack discretion,
Yet are they passing cowardly. But, I beseech you,
What says the other troop?

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: They are dissolved: hang 'em!
They said they were an-hungry; sigh'd forth proverbs,
That hunger broke stone walls, that dogs must eat,
That meat was made for mouths, that the gods sent not
Corn for the rich men only: with these shreds
They vented their complainings; which being answer'd,
And a petition granted them, a strange one--
To break the heart of generosity,
And make bold power look pale--they threw their caps
As they would hang them on the horns o' the moon,
Shouting their emulation.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: What is granted them?

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Five tribunes to defend their vulgar wisdoms,
Of their own choice: one's Junius Brutus,
Sicinius Velutus, and I know not--'Sdeath!
The rabble should have first unroof'd the city,
Ere so prevail'd with me: it will in time
Win upon power and throw forth greater themes
For insurrection's arguing.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: This is strange.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Go, get you home, you fragments!

units.speaker: Messenger
lines: Where's Caius Marcius?

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Here: what's the matter?

units.speaker: Messenger
lines: The news is, sir, the Volsces are in arms.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: I am glad on 't: then we shall ha' means to vent
Our musty superfluity. See, our best elders.

units.text: Enter COMINIUS, TITUS LARTIUS, and other Senators; JUNIUS BRUTUS and SICINIUS VELUTUS

units.speaker: First Senator
lines: Marcius, 'tis true that you have lately told us;
The Volsces are in arms.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: They have a leader,
Tullus Aufidius, that will put you to 't.
I sin in envying his nobility,
And were I any thing but what I am,
I would wish me only he.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: You have fought together.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Were half to half the world by the ears and he.
Upon my party, I'ld revolt to make
Only my wars with him: he is a lion
That I am proud to hunt.

units.speaker: First Senator
lines: Then, worthy Marcius,
Attend upon Cominius to these wars.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: It is your former promise.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Sir, it is;
And I am constant. Titus Lartius, thou
Shalt see me once more strike at Tullus' face.
What, art thou stiff? stand'st out?

units.speaker: TITUS
lines: No, Caius Marcius;
I'll lean upon one crutch and fight with t'other,
Ere stay behind this business.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: O, true-bred!

units.speaker: First Senator
lines: Your company to the Capitol; where, I know,
Our greatest friends attend us.

units.speaker: TITUS
lines: [To COMINIUS]                Lead you on.
Right worthy you priority.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Noble Marcius!

units.speaker: First Senator
lines: [To the Citizens]  Hence to your homes; be gone!

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Nay, let them follow:
The Volsces have much corn; take these rats thither
To gnaw their garners. Worshipful mutiners,
Your valour puts well forth: pray, follow.

units.text: Citizens steal away. Exeunt all but SICINIUS and BRUTUS

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Was ever man so proud as is this Marcius?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: He has no equal.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: When we were chosen tribunes for the people,--

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Mark'd you his lip and eyes?

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Nay. but his taunts.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Being moved, he will not spare to gird the gods.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Be-mock the modest moon.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: The present wars devour him: he is grown
Too proud to be so valiant.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Such a nature,
Tickled with good success, disdains the shadow
Which he treads on at noon: but I do wonder
His insolence can brook to be commanded
Under Cominius.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Fame, at the which he aims,
In whom already he's well graced, can not
Better be held nor more attain'd than by
A place below the first: for what miscarries
Shall be the general's fault, though he perform
To the utmost of a man, and giddy censure
Will then cry out of Marcius 'O if he
Had borne the business!'

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Besides, if things go well,
Opinion that so sticks on Marcius shall
Of his demerits rob Cominius.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Come:
Half all Cominius' honours are to Marcius.
Though Marcius earned them not, and all his faults
To Marcius shall be honours, though indeed
In aught he merit not.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Let's hence, and hear
How the dispatch is made, and in what fashion,
More than his singularity, he goes
Upon this present action.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Lets along.

units.text: Enter TULLUS AUFIDIUS and certain Senators

units.speaker: First Senator
lines: So, your opinion is, Aufidius,
That they of Rome are entered in our counsels
And know how we proceed.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
lines: Is it not yours?
What ever have been thought on in this state,
That could be brought to bodily act ere Rome
Had circumvention? 'Tis not four days gone
Since I heard thence; these are the words: I think
I have the letter here; yes, here it is.
'They have press'd a power, but it is not known
Whether for east or west: the dearth is great;
The people mutinous; and it is rumour'd,
Cominius, Marcius your old enemy,
Who is of Rome worse hated than of you,
And Titus Lartius, a most valiant Roman,
These three lead on this preparation
Whither 'tis bent: most likely 'tis for you:
Consider of it.'

units.speaker: First Senator
lines: Our army's in the field
We never yet made doubt but Rome was ready
To answer us.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
lines: Nor did you think it folly
To keep your great pretences veil'd till when
They needs must show themselves; which
in the hatching,
It seem'd, appear'd to Rome. By the discovery.
We shall be shorten'd in our aim, which was
To take in many towns ere almost Rome
Should know we were afoot.

units.speaker: Second Senator
lines: Noble Aufidius,
Take your commission; hie you to your bands:
Let us alone to guard Corioli:
If they set down before 's, for the remove
Bring your army; but, I think, you'll find
They've not prepared for us.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
lines: O, doubt not that;
I speak from certainties. Nay, more,
Some parcels of their power are forth already,
And only hitherward. I leave your honours.
If we and Caius Marcius chance to meet,
'Tis sworn between us we shall ever strike
Till one can do no more.

units.speaker: All
lines: The gods assist you!

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
lines: And keep your honours safe!

units.speaker: First Senator
lines: Farewell.

units.speaker: Second Senator
lines: Farewell.

units.speaker: All
lines: Farewell.

scenes.setting: Rome. A room in Marcius' house.

units.text: Enter VOLUMNIA and VIRGILIA	they set them down on two low stools, and sew

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: I pray you, daughter, sing; or express yourself in a
more comfortable sort: if my son were my husband, I
should freelier rejoice in that absence wherein he
won honour than in the embracements of his bed where
he would show most love. When yet he was but
tender-bodied and the only son of my womb, when
youth with comeliness plucked all gaze his way, when
for a day of kings' entreaties a mother should not
sell him an hour from her beholding, I, considering
how honour would become such a person. that it was
no better than picture-like to hang by the wall, if
renown made it not stir, was pleased to let him seek
danger where he was like to find fame. To a cruel
war I sent him; from whence he returned, his brows
bound with oak. I tell thee, daughter, I sprang not
more in joy at first hearing he was a man-child
than now in first seeing he had proved himself a
man.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: But had he died in the business, madam; how then?

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: Then his good report should have been my son; I
therein would have found issue. Hear me profess
sincerely: had I a dozen sons, each in my love
alike and none less dear than thine and my good
Marcius, I had rather had eleven die nobly for their
country than one voluptuously surfeit out of action.

units.speaker: Gentlewoman
lines: Madam, the Lady Valeria is come to visit you.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: Beseech you, give me leave to retire myself.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: Indeed, you shall not.
Methinks I hear hither your husband's drum,
See him pluck Aufidius down by the hair,
As children from a bear, the Volsces shunning him:
Methinks I see him stamp thus, and call thus:
'Come on, you cowards! you were got in fear,
Though you were born in Rome:' his bloody brow
With his mail'd hand then wiping, forth he goes,
Like to a harvest-man that's task'd to mow
Or all or lose his hire.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: His bloody brow! O Jupiter, no blood!

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: Away, you fool! it more becomes a man
Than gilt his trophy: the breasts of Hecuba,
When she did suckle Hector, look'd not lovelier
Than Hector's forehead when it spit forth blood
At Grecian sword, contemning. Tell Valeria,
We are fit to bid her welcome.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: Heavens bless my lord from fell Aufidius!

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: He'll beat Aufidius 'head below his knee
And tread upon his neck.

units.text: Enter VALERIA, with an Usher and Gentlewoman

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: My ladies both, good day to you.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: Sweet madam.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: I am glad to see your ladyship.

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: How do you both? you are manifest house-keepers.
What are you sewing here? A fine spot, in good
faith. How does your little son?

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: I thank your ladyship; well, good madam.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: He had rather see the swords, and hear a drum, than
look upon his school-master.

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: O' my word, the father's son: I'll swear,'tis a
very pretty boy. O' my troth, I looked upon him o'
Wednesday half an hour together: has such a
confirmed countenance. I saw him run after a gilded
butterfly: and when he caught it, he let it go
again; and after it again; and over and over he
comes, and again; catched it again; or whether his
fall enraged him, or how 'twas, he did so set his
teeth and tear it; O, I warrant it, how he mammocked
it!

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: One on 's father's moods.

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: Indeed, la, 'tis a noble child.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: A crack, madam.

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: Come, lay aside your stitchery; I must have you play
the idle husewife with me this afternoon.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: No, good madam; I will not out of doors.

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: Not out of doors!

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: She shall, she shall.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: Indeed, no, by your patience; I'll not over the
threshold till my lord return from the wars.

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: Fie, you confine yourself most unreasonably: come,
you must go visit the good lady that lies in.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: I will wish her speedy strength, and visit her with
my prayers; but I cannot go thither.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: Why, I pray you?

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: 'Tis not to save labour, nor that I want love.

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: You would be another Penelope: yet, they say, all
the yarn she spun in Ulysses' absence did but fill
Ithaca full of moths. Come; I would your cambric
were sensible as your finger, that you might leave
pricking it for pity. Come, you shall go with us.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: No, good madam, pardon me; indeed, I will not forth.

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: In truth, la, go with me; and I'll tell you
excellent news of your husband.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: O, good madam, there can be none yet.

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: Verily, I do not jest with you; there came news from
him last night.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: Indeed, madam?

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: In earnest, it's true; I heard a senator speak it.
Thus it is: the Volsces have an army forth; against
whom Cominius the general is gone, with one part of
our Roman power: your lord and Titus Lartius are set
down before their city Corioli; they nothing doubt
prevailing and to make it brief wars. This is true,
on mine honour; and so, I pray, go with us.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: Give me excuse, good madam; I will obey you in every
thing hereafter.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: Let her alone, lady: as she is now, she will but
disease our better mirth.

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: In troth, I think she would. Fare you well, then.
Come, good sweet lady. Prithee, Virgilia, turn thy
solemness out o' door. and go along with us.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: No, at a word, madam; indeed, I must not. I wish
you much mirth.

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: Well, then, farewell.

units.text: Enter, with drum and colours, MARCIUS, TITUS  LARTIUS, Captains and Soldiers. To them a Messenger

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Yonder comes news. A wager they have met.

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: My horse to yours, no.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: 'Tis done.

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: Agreed.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Say, has our general met the enemy?

units.speaker: Messenger
lines: They lie in view; but have not spoke as yet.

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: So, the good horse is mine.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: I'll buy him of you.

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: No, I'll nor sell nor give him: lend you him I will
For half a hundred years. Summon the town.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: How far off lie these armies?

units.speaker: Messenger
lines: Within this mile and half.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Then shall we hear their 'larum, and they ours.
Now, Mars, I prithee, make us quick in work,
That we with smoking swords may march from hence,
To help our fielded friends! Come, blow thy blast.

units.text: They sound a parley. Enter two Senators with others on the walls

lines: Tutus Aufidius, is he within your walls?

units.speaker: First Senator
lines: No, nor a man that fears you less than he,
That's lesser than a little.
Hark! our drums
Are bringing forth our youth. We'll break our walls,
Rather than they shall pound us up: our gates,
Which yet seem shut, we, have but pinn'd with rushes;
They'll open of themselves.
Hark you. far off!
There is Aufidius; list, what work he makes
Amongst your cloven army.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: O, they are at it!

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: Their noise be our instruction. Ladders, ho!

units.text: Enter the army of the Volsces

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: They fear us not, but issue forth their city.
Now put your shields before your hearts, and fight
With hearts more proof than shields. Advance,
brave Titus:
They do disdain us much beyond our thoughts,
Which makes me sweat with wrath. Come on, my fellows:
He that retires I'll take him for a Volsce,
And he shall feel mine edge.

units.text: Alarum. The Romans are beat back to their trenches. Re-enter MARCIUS cursing

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: All the contagion of the south light on you,
You shames of Rome! you herd of--Boils and plagues
Plaster you o'er, that you may be abhorr'd
Further than seen and one infect another
Against the wind a mile! You souls of geese,
That bear the shapes of men, how have you run
From slaves that apes would beat! Pluto and hell!
All hurt behind; backs red, and faces pale
With flight and agued fear! Mend and charge home,
Or, by the fires of heaven, I'll leave the foe
And make my wars on you: look to't: come on;
If you'll stand fast, we'll beat them to their wives,
As they us to our trenches followed.

units.text: Another alarum. The Volsces fly, and MARCIUS follows them to the gates

lines: So, now the gates are ope: now prove good seconds:
'Tis for the followers fortune widens them,
Not for the fliers: mark me, and do the like.

units.speaker: First Soldier
lines: Fool-hardiness; not I.

units.speaker: Second Soldier
lines: Nor I.

units.speaker: First Soldier
lines: See, they have shut him in.

units.speaker: All
lines: To the pot, I warrant him.

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: What is become of Marcius?

units.speaker: All
lines: Slain, sir, doubtless.

units.speaker: First Soldier
lines: Following the fliers at the very heels,
With them he enters; who, upon the sudden,
Clapp'd to their gates: he is himself alone,
To answer all the city.

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: O noble fellow!
Who sensibly outdares his senseless sword,
And, when it bows, stands up. Thou art left, Marcius:
A carbuncle entire, as big as thou art,
Were not so rich a jewel. Thou wast a soldier
Even to Cato's wish, not fierce and terrible
Only in strokes; but, with thy grim looks and
The thunder-like percussion of thy sounds,
Thou madst thine enemies shake, as if the world
Were feverous and did tremble.

units.text: Re-enter MARCIUS, bleeding, assaulted by the enemy

units.speaker: First Soldier
lines: Look, sir.

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: O,'tis Marcius!
Let's fetch him off, or make remain alike.

units.text: They fight, and all enter the city

units.speaker: First Roman
lines: This will I carry to Rome.

units.speaker: Second Roman
lines: And I this.

units.speaker: Third Roman
lines: A murrain on't! I took this for silver.

units.text: Enter MARCIUS and TITUS LARTIUS with a trumpet

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: See here these movers that do prize their hours
At a crack'd drachm! Cushions, leaden spoons,
Irons of a doit, doublets that hangmen would
Bury with those that wore them, these base slaves,
Ere yet the fight be done, pack up: down with them!
And hark, what noise the general makes! To him!
There is the man of my soul's hate, Aufidius,
Piercing our Romans: then, valiant Titus, take
Convenient numbers to make good the city;
Whilst I, with those that have the spirit, will haste
To help Cominius.

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: Worthy sir, thou bleed'st;
Thy exercise hath been too violent for
A second course of fight.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Sir, praise me not;
My work hath yet not warm'd me: fare you well:
The blood I drop is rather physical
Than dangerous to me: to Aufidius thus
I will appear, and fight.

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: Now the fair goddess, Fortune,
Fall deep in love with thee; and her great charms
Misguide thy opposers' swords! Bold gentleman,
Prosperity be thy page!

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Thy friend no less
Than those she placeth highest! So, farewell.

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: Thou worthiest Marcius!
Go, sound thy trumpet in the market-place;
Call thither all the officers o' the town,
Where they shall know our mind: away!

units.text: Enter COMINIUS, as it were in retire, with soldiers

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Breathe you, my friends: well fought;
we are come off
Like Romans, neither foolish in our stands,
Nor cowardly in retire: believe me, sirs,
We shall be charged again. Whiles we have struck,
By interims and conveying gusts we have heard
The charges of our friends. Ye Roman gods!
Lead their successes as we wish our own,
That both our powers, with smiling
fronts encountering,
May give you thankful sacrifice.
Thy news?

units.speaker: Messenger
lines: The citizens of Corioli have issued,
And given to Lartius and to Marcius battle:
I saw our party to their trenches driven,
And then I came away.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Though thou speak'st truth,
Methinks thou speak'st not well.
How long is't since?

units.speaker: Messenger
lines: Above an hour, my lord.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: 'Tis not a mile; briefly we heard their drums:
How couldst thou in a mile confound an hour,
And bring thy news so late?

units.speaker: Messenger
lines: Spies of the Volsces
Held me in chase, that I was forced to wheel
Three or four miles about, else had I, sir,
Half an hour since brought my report.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Who's yonder,
That does appear as he were flay'd? O gods
He has the stamp of Marcius; and I have
Before-time seen him thus.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: [Within]                 Come I too late?

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: The shepherd knows not thunder from a tabour
More than I know the sound of Marcius' tongue
From every meaner man.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Come I too late?

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Ay, if you come not in the blood of others,
But mantled in your own.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: O, let me clip ye
In arms as sound as when I woo'd, in heart
As merry as when our nuptial day was done,
And tapers burn'd to bedward!

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Flower of warriors,
How is it with Titus Lartius?

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: As with a man busied about decrees:
Condemning some to death, and some to exile;
Ransoming him, or pitying, threatening the other;
Holding Corioli in the name of Rome,
Even like a fawning greyhound in the leash,
To let him slip at will.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Where is that slave
Which told me they had beat you to your trenches?
Where is he? call him hither.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Let him alone;
He did inform the truth: but for our gentlemen,
The common file--a plague! tribunes for them!--
The mouse ne'er shunn'd the cat as they did budge
From rascals worse than they.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: But how prevail'd you?

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Will the time serve to tell? I do not think.
Where is the enemy? are you lords o' the field?
If not, why cease you till you are so?

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Marcius,
We have at disadvantage fought and did
Retire to win our purpose.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: How lies their battle? know you on which side
They have placed their men of trust?

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: As I guess, Marcius,
Their bands i' the vaward are the Antiates,
Of their best trust; o'er them Aufidius,
Their very heart of hope.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: I do beseech you,
By all the battles wherein we have fought,
By the blood we have shed together, by the vows
We have made to endure friends, that you directly
Set me against Aufidius and his Antiates;
And that you not delay the present, but,
Filling the air with swords advanced and darts,
We prove this very hour.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Though I could wish
You were conducted to a gentle bath
And balms applied to, you, yet dare I never
Deny your asking: take your choice of those
That best can aid your action.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Those are they
That most are willing. If any such be here--
As it were sin to doubt--that love this painting
Wherein you see me smear'd; if any fear
Lesser his person than an ill report;
If any think brave death outweighs bad life
And that his country's dearer than himself;
Let him alone, or so many so minded,
Wave thus, to express his disposition,
And follow Marcius.

units.text: They all shout and wave their swords, take him up in their arms, and cast up their caps

lines: O, me alone! make you a sword of me?
If these shows be not outward, which of you
But is four Volsces? none of you but is
Able to bear against the great Aufidius
A shield as hard as his. A certain number,
Though thanks to all, must I select
from all: the rest
Shall bear the business in some other fight,
As cause will be obey'd. Please you to march;
And four shall quickly draw out my command,
Which men are best inclined.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: March on, my fellows:
Make good this ostentation, and you shall
Divide in all with us.

units.text: TITUS LARTIUS, having set a guard upon  Corioli, going with drum and trumpet toward  COMINIUS and CAIUS MARCIUS, enters with Lieutenant, other Soldiers, and a Scout

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: So, let the ports be guarded: keep your duties,
As I have set them down. If I do send, dispatch
Those centuries to our aid: the rest will serve
For a short holding: if we lose the field,
We cannot keep the town.

units.speaker: Lieutenant
lines: Fear not our care, sir.

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: Hence, and shut your gates upon's.
Our guider, come; to the Roman camp conduct us.

units.text: Alarum as in battle. Enter, from opposite sides, MARCIUS and AUFIDIUS

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: I'll fight with none but thee; for I do hate thee
Worse than a promise-breaker.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
lines: We hate alike:
Not Afric owns a serpent I abhor
More than thy fame and envy. Fix thy foot.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Let the first budger die the other's slave,
And the gods doom him after!

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
lines: If I fly, Marcius,
Holloa me like a hare.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Within these three hours, Tullus,
Alone I fought in your Corioli walls,
And made what work I pleased: 'tis not my blood
Wherein thou seest me mask'd; for thy revenge
Wrench up thy power to the highest.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
lines: Wert thou the Hector
That was the whip of your bragg'd progeny,
Thou shouldst not scape me here.

units.text: They fight, and certain Volsces come to the aid of  AUFIDIUS. MARCIUS fights till they be driven in breathless

lines: Officious, and not valiant, you have shamed me
In your condemned seconds.

units.text: Flourish. Alarum. A retreat is sounded. Flourish.  Enter, from one side, COMINIUS with the Romans; from the other side, MARCIUS, with his arm in a scarf

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: If I should tell thee o'er this thy day's work,
Thou'ldst not believe thy deeds: but I'll report it
Where senators shall mingle tears with smiles,
Where great patricians shall attend and shrug,
I' the end admire, where ladies shall be frighted,
And, gladly quaked, hear more; where the
dull tribunes,
That, with the fusty plebeians, hate thine honours,
Shall say against their hearts 'We thank the gods
Our Rome hath such a soldier.'
Yet camest thou to a morsel of this feast,
Having fully dined before.

units.text: Enter TITUS LARTIUS, with his power, from the pursuit

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: O general,
Here is the steed, we the caparison:
Hadst thou beheld--

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: Pray now, no more: my mother,
Who has a charter to extol her blood,
When she does praise me grieves me. I have done
As you have done; that's what I can; induced
As you have been; that's for my country:
He that has but effected his good will
Hath overta'en mine act.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: You shall not be
The grave of your deserving; Rome must know
The value of her own: 'twere a concealment
Worse than a theft, no less than a traducement,
To hide your doings; and to silence that,
Which, to the spire and top of praises vouch'd,
Would seem but modest: therefore, I beseech you
In sign of what you are, not to reward
What you have done--before our army hear me.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: I have some wounds upon me, and they smart
To hear themselves remember'd.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Should they not,
Well might they fester 'gainst ingratitude,
And tent themselves with death. Of all the horses,
Whereof we have ta'en good and good store, of all
The treasure in this field achieved and city,
We render you the tenth, to be ta'en forth,
Before the common distribution, at
Your only choice.

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: I thank you, general;
But cannot make my heart consent to take
A bribe to pay my sword: I do refuse it;
And stand upon my common part with those
That have beheld the doing.

units.text: A long flourish. They all cry 'Marcius! Marcius!'  cast up their caps and lances: COMINIUS and LARTIUS stand bare

units.speaker: MARCIUS
lines: May these same instruments, which you profane,
Never sound more! when drums and trumpets shall
I' the field prove flatterers, let courts and cities be
Made all of false-faced soothing!
When steel grows soft as the parasite's silk,
Let him be made a coverture for the wars!
No more, I say! For that I have not wash'd
My nose that bled, or foil'd some debile wretch.--
Which, without note, here's many else have done,--
You shout me forth
In acclamations hyperbolical;
As if I loved my little should be dieted
In praises sauced with lies.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Too modest are you;
More cruel to your good report than grateful
To us that give you truly: by your patience,
If 'gainst yourself you be incensed, we'll put you,
Like one that means his proper harm, in manacles,
Then reason safely with you. Therefore, be it known,
As to us, to all the world, that Caius Marcius
Wears this war's garland: in token of the which,
My noble steed, known to the camp, I give him,
With all his trim belonging; and from this time,
For what he did before Corioli, call him,
With all the applause and clamour of the host,
CAIUS MARCIUS CORIOLANUS! Bear
The addition nobly ever!

units.speaker: All
lines: Caius Marcius Coriolanus!

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: I will go wash;
And when my face is fair, you shall perceive
Whether I blush or no: howbeit, I thank you.
I mean to stride your steed, and at all times
To undercrest your good addition
To the fairness of my power.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: So, to our tent;
Where, ere we do repose us, we will write
To Rome of our success. You, Titus Lartius,
Must to Corioli back: send us to Rome
The best, with whom we may articulate,
For their own good and ours.

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: I shall, my lord.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: The gods begin to mock me. I, that now
Refused most princely gifts, am bound to beg
Of my lord general.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Take't; 'tis yours. What is't?

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: I sometime lay here in Corioli
At a poor man's house; he used me kindly:
He cried to me; I saw him prisoner;
But then Aufidius was with in my view,
And wrath o'erwhelm'd my pity: I request you
To give my poor host freedom.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: O, well begg'd!
Were he the butcher of my son, he should
Be free as is the wind. Deliver him, Titus.

units.speaker: LARTIUS
lines: Marcius, his name?

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: By Jupiter! forgot.
I am weary; yea, my memory is tired.
Have we no wine here?

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Go we to our tent:
The blood upon your visage dries; 'tis time
It should be look'd to: come.

units.text: A flourish. Cornets. Enter TULLUS AUFIDIUS, bloody, with two or three Soldiers

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
lines: The town is ta'en!

units.speaker: First Soldier
lines: 'Twill be deliver'd back on good condition.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
lines: Condition!
I would I were a Roman; for I cannot,
Being a Volsce, be that I am. Condition!
What good condition can a treaty find
I' the part that is at mercy? Five times, Marcius,
I have fought with thee: so often hast thou beat me,
And wouldst do so, I think, should we encounter
As often as we eat. By the elements,
If e'er again I meet him beard to beard,
He's mine, or I am his: mine emulation
Hath not that honour in't it had; for where
I thought to crush him in an equal force,
True sword to sword, I'll potch at him some way
Or wrath or craft may get him.

units.speaker: First Soldier
lines: He's the devil.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
lines: Bolder, though not so subtle. My valour's poison'd
With only suffering stain by him; for him
Shall fly out of itself: nor sleep nor sanctuary,
Being naked, sick, nor fane nor Capitol,
The prayers of priests nor times of sacrifice,
Embarquements all of fury, shall lift up
Their rotten privilege and custom 'gainst
My hate to Marcius: where I find him, were it
At home, upon my brother's guard, even there,
Against the hospitable canon, would I
Wash my fierce hand in's heart. Go you to the city;
Learn how 'tis held; and what they are that must
Be hostages for Rome.

units.speaker: First Soldier
lines: Will not you go?

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
lines: I am attended at the cypress grove: I pray you--
'Tis south the city mills--bring me word thither
How the world goes, that to the pace of it
I may spur on my journey.

units.speaker: First Soldier
lines: I shall, sir.

units.text: Enter MENENIUS with the two Tribunes of the people, SICINIUS and BRUTUS.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: The augurer tells me we shall have news to-night.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Good or bad?

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Not according to the prayer of the people, for they
love not Marcius.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Nature teaches beasts to know their friends.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Pray you, who does the wolf love?

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: The lamb.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Ay, to devour him; as the hungry plebeians would the
noble Marcius.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: He's a lamb indeed, that baes like a bear.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: He's a bear indeed, that lives like a lamb. You two
are old men: tell me one thing that I shall ask you.

units.speaker: Both
lines: Well, sir.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: In what enormity is Marcius poor in, that you two
have not in abundance?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: He's poor in no one fault, but stored with all.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Especially in pride.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: And topping all others in boasting.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: This is strange now: do you two know how you are
censured here in the city, I mean of us o' the
right-hand file? do you?

units.speaker: Both
lines: Why, how are we censured?

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Because you talk of pride now,--will you not be angry?

units.speaker: Both
lines: Well, well, sir, well.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Why, 'tis no great matter; for a very little thief of
occasion will rob you of a great deal of patience:
give your dispositions the reins, and be angry at
your pleasures; at the least if you take it as a
pleasure to you in being so. You blame Marcius for
being proud?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: We do it not alone, sir.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: I know you can do very little alone; for your helps
are many, or else your actions would grow wondrous
single: your abilities are too infant-like for
doing much alone. You talk of pride: O that you
could turn your eyes toward the napes of your necks,
and make but an interior survey of your good selves!
O that you could!

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: What then, sir?

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Why, then you should discover a brace of unmeriting,
proud, violent, testy magistrates, alias fools, as
any in Rome.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Menenius, you are known well enough too.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: I am known to be a humorous patrician, and one that
loves a cup of hot wine with not a drop of allaying
Tiber in't; said to be something imperfect in
favouring the first complaint; hasty and tinder-like
upon too trivial motion; one that converses more
with the buttock of the night than with the forehead
of the morning: what I think I utter, and spend my
malice in my breath. Meeting two such wealsmen as
you are--I cannot call you Lycurguses--if the drink
you give me touch my palate adversely, I make a
crooked face at it. I can't say your worships have
delivered the matter well, when I find the ass in
compound with the major part of your syllables: and
though I must be content to bear with those that say
you are reverend grave men, yet they lie deadly that
tell you you have good faces. If you see this in
the map of my microcosm, follows it that I am known
well enough too? what barm can your bisson
conspectuities glean out of this character, if I be
known well enough too?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Come, sir, come, we know you well enough.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: You know neither me, yourselves nor any thing. You
are ambitious for poor knaves' caps and legs: you
wear out a good wholesome forenoon in hearing a
cause between an orange wife and a fosset-seller;
and then rejourn the controversy of three pence to a
second day of audience. When you are hearing a
matter between party and party, if you chance to be
pinched with the colic, you make faces like
mummers; set up the bloody flag against all
patience; and, in roaring for a chamber-pot,
dismiss the controversy bleeding the more entangled
by your hearing: all the peace you make in their
cause is, calling both the parties knaves. You are
a pair of strange ones.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Come, come, you are well understood to be a
perfecter giber for the table than a necessary
bencher in the Capitol.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Our very priests must become mockers, if they shall
encounter such ridiculous subjects as you are. When
you speak best unto the purpose, it is not worth the
wagging of your beards; and your beards deserve not
so honourable a grave as to stuff a botcher's
cushion, or to be entombed in an ass's pack-
saddle. Yet you must be saying, Marcius is proud;
who in a cheap estimation, is worth predecessors
since Deucalion, though peradventure some of the
best of 'em were hereditary hangmen. God-den to
your worships: more of your conversation would
infect my brain, being the herdsmen of the beastly
plebeians: I will be bold to take my leave of you.
How now, my as fair as noble ladies,--and the moon,
were she earthly, no nobler,--whither do you follow
your eyes so fast?

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: Honourable Menenius, my boy Marcius approaches; for
the love of Juno, let's go.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Ha! Marcius coming home!

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: Ay, worthy Menenius; and with most prosperous
approbation.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Take my cap, Jupiter, and I thank thee. Hoo!
Marcius coming home!

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: Nay,'tis true.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: Look, here's a letter from him: the state hath
another, his wife another; and, I think, there's one
at home for you.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: I will make my very house reel tonight: a letter for
me!

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: Yes, certain, there's a letter for you; I saw't.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: A letter for me! it gives me an estate of seven
years' health; in which time I will make a lip at
the physician: the most sovereign prescription in
Galen is but empiricutic, and, to this preservative,
of no better report than a horse-drench. Is he
not wounded? he was wont to come home wounded.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: O, no, no, no.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: O, he is wounded; I thank the gods for't.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: So do I too, if it be not too much: brings a'
victory in his pocket? the wounds become him.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: On's brows: Menenius, he comes the third time home
with the oaken garland.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Has he disciplined Aufidius soundly?

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: Titus Lartius writes, they fought together, but
Aufidius got off.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: And 'twas time for him too, I'll warrant him that:
an he had stayed by him, I would not have been so
fidiused for all the chests in Corioli, and the gold
that's in them. Is the senate possessed of this?

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: Good ladies, let's go. Yes, yes, yes; the senate
has letters from the general, wherein he gives my
son the whole name of the war: he hath in this
action outdone his former deeds doubly

units.speaker: VALERIA
lines: In troth, there's wondrous things spoke of him.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Wondrous! ay, I warrant you, and not without his
true purchasing.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
lines: The gods grant them true!

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: True! pow, wow.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: True! I'll be sworn they are true.
Where is he wounded?
God save your good worships! Marcius is coming
home: he has more cause to be proud. Where is he wounded?

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: I' the shoulder and i' the left arm there will be
large cicatrices to show the people, when he shall
stand for his place. He received in the repulse of
Tarquin seven hurts i' the body.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: One i' the neck, and two i' the thigh,--there's
nine that I know.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: He had, before this last expedition, twenty-five
wounds upon him.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Now it's twenty-seven: every gash was an enemy's grave.
Hark! the trumpets.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: These are the ushers of Marcius: before him he
carries noise, and behind him he leaves tears:
Death, that dark spirit, in 's nervy arm doth lie;
Which, being advanced, declines, and then men die.

units.text: A sennet. Trumpets sound. Enter COMINIUS the  general, and TITUS LARTIUS; between them, CORIOLANUS,  crowned with an oaken garland; with Captains and Soldiers, and a Herald

units.speaker: Herald
lines: Know, Rome, that all alone Marcius did fight
Within Corioli gates: where he hath won,
With fame, a name to Caius Marcius; these
In honour follows Coriolanus.
Welcome to Rome, renowned Coriolanus!

units.speaker: All
lines: Welcome to Rome, renowned Coriolanus!

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: No more of this; it does offend my heart:
Pray now, no more.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Look, sir, your mother!

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: O,
You have, I know, petition'd all the gods
For my prosperity!

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: Nay, my good soldier, up;
My gentle Marcius, worthy Caius, and
By deed-achieving honour newly named,--
What is it?--Coriolanus must I call thee?--
But O, thy wife!

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: My gracious silence, hail!
Wouldst thou have laugh'd had I come coffin'd home,
That weep'st to see me triumph? Ay, my dear,
Such eyes the widows in Corioli wear,
And mothers that lack sons.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Now, the gods crown thee!

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: And live you yet?
O my sweet lady, pardon.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: I know not where to turn: O, welcome home:
And welcome, general: and ye're welcome all.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: A hundred thousand welcomes. I could weep
And I could laugh, I am light and heavy. Welcome.
A curse begin at very root on's heart,
That is not glad to see thee! You are three
That Rome should dote on: yet, by the faith of men,
We have some old crab-trees here
at home that will not
Be grafted to your relish. Yet welcome, warriors:
We call a nettle but a nettle and
The faults of fools but folly.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Ever right.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Menenius ever, ever.

units.speaker: Herald
lines: Give way there, and go on!

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: [To VOLUMNIA and VIRGILIA]  Your hand, and yours:
Ere in our own house I do shade my head,
The good patricians must be visited;
From whom I have received not only greetings,
But with them change of honours.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
lines: I have lived
To see inherited my very wishes
And the buildings of my fancy: only
There's one thing wanting, which I doubt not but
Our Rome will cast upon thee.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Know, good mother,
I had rather be their servant in my way,
Than sway with them in theirs.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: On, to the Capitol!

units.text: Flourish. Cornets. Exeunt in state, as before. BRUTUS and SICINIUS come forward

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: All tongues speak of him, and the bleared sights
Are spectacled to see him: your prattling nurse
Into a rapture lets her baby cry
While she chats him: the kitchen malkin pins
Her richest lockram 'bout her reechy neck,
Clambering the walls to eye him: stalls, bulks, windows,
Are smother'd up, leads fill'd, and ridges horsed
With variable complexions, all agreeing
In earnestness to see him: seld-shown flamens
Do press among the popular throngs and puff
To win a vulgar station: or veil'd dames
Commit the war of white and damask in
Their nicely-gawded cheeks to the wanton spoil
Of Phoebus' burning kisses: such a pother
As if that whatsoever god who leads him
Were slily crept into his human powers
And gave him graceful posture.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: On the sudden,
I warrant him consul.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Then our office may,
During his power, go sleep.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: He cannot temperately transport his honours
From where he should begin and end, but will
Lose those he hath won.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: In that there's comfort.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Doubt not
The commoners, for whom we stand, but they
Upon their ancient malice will forget
With the least cause these his new honours, which
That he will give them make I as little question
As he is proud to do't.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: I heard him swear,
Were he to stand for consul, never would he
Appear i' the market-place nor on him put
The napless vesture of humility;
Nor showing, as the manner is, his wounds
To the people, beg their stinking breaths.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: 'Tis right.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: It was his word: O, he would miss it rather
Than carry it but by the suit of the gentry to him,
And the desire of the nobles.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: I wish no better
Than have him hold that purpose and to put it
In execution.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: 'Tis most like he will.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: It shall be to him then as our good wills,
A sure destruction.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: So it must fall out
To him or our authorities. For an end,
We must suggest the people in what hatred
He still hath held them; that to's power he would
Have made them mules, silenced their pleaders and
Dispropertied their freedoms, holding them,
In human action and capacity,
Of no more soul nor fitness for the world
Than camels in the war, who have their provand
Only for bearing burdens, and sore blows
For sinking under them.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: This, as you say, suggested
At some time when his soaring insolence
Shall touch the people--which time shall not want,
If he be put upon 't; and that's as easy
As to set dogs on sheep--will be his fire
To kindle their dry stubble; and their blaze
Shall darken him for ever.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: What's the matter?

units.speaker: Messenger
lines: You are sent for to the Capitol. 'Tis thought
That Marcius shall be consul:
I have seen the dumb men throng to see him and
The blind to bear him speak: matrons flung gloves,
Ladies and maids their scarfs and handkerchers,
Upon him as he pass'd: the nobles bended,
As to Jove's statue, and the commons made
A shower and thunder with their caps and shouts:
I never saw the like.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Let's to the Capitol;
And carry with us ears and eyes for the time,
But hearts for the event.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Have with you.

units.text: Enter two Officers, to lay cushions

units.speaker: First Officer
lines: Come, come, they are almost here. How many stand
for consulships?

units.speaker: Second Officer
lines: Three, they say: but 'tis thought of every one
Coriolanus will carry it.

units.speaker: First Officer
lines: That's a brave fellow; but he's vengeance proud, and
loves not the common people.

units.speaker: Second Officer
lines: Faith, there had been many great men that have
flattered the people, who ne'er loved them; and there
be many that they have loved, they know not
wherefore: so that, if they love they know not why,
they hate upon no better a ground: therefore, for
Coriolanus neither to care whether they love or hate
him manifests the true knowledge he has in their
disposition; and out of his noble carelessness lets
them plainly see't.

units.speaker: First Officer
lines: If he did not care whether he had their love or no,
he waved indifferently 'twixt doing them neither
good nor harm: but he seeks their hate with greater
devotion than can render it him; and leaves
nothing undone that may fully discover him their
opposite. Now, to seem to affect the malice and
displeasure of the people is as bad as that which he
dislikes, to flatter them for their love.

units.speaker: Second Officer
lines: He hath deserved worthily of his country: and his
ascent is not by such easy degrees as those who,
having been supple and courteous to the people,
bonneted, without any further deed to have them at
an into their estimation and report: but he hath so
planted his honours in their eyes, and his actions
in their hearts, that for their tongues to be
silent, and not confess so much, were a kind of
ingrateful injury; to report otherwise, were a
malice, that, giving itself the lie, would pluck
reproof and rebuke from every ear that heard it.

units.speaker: First Officer
lines: No more of him; he is a worthy man: make way, they
are coming.

units.text: A sennet. Enter, with actors before them, COMINIUS  the consul, MENENIUS, CORIOLANUS, Senators,  SICINIUS and BRUTUS. The Senators take their  places; the Tribunes take their Places by themselves. CORIOLANUS stands

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Having determined of the Volsces and
To send for Titus Lartius, it remains,
As the main point of this our after-meeting,
To gratify his noble service that
Hath thus stood for his country: therefore,
please you,
Most reverend and grave elders, to desire
The present consul, and last general
In our well-found successes, to report
A little of that worthy work perform'd
By Caius Marcius Coriolanus, whom
We met here both to thank and to remember
With honours like himself.

units.speaker: First Senator
lines: Speak, good Cominius:
Leave nothing out for length, and make us think
Rather our state's defective for requital
Than we to stretch it out.
Masters o' the people,
We do request your kindest ears, and after,
Your loving motion toward the common body,
To yield what passes here.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: We are convented
Upon a pleasing treaty, and have hearts
Inclinable to honour and advance
The theme of our assembly.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Which the rather
We shall be blest to do, if he remember
A kinder value of the people than
He hath hereto prized them at.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: That's off, that's off;
I would you rather had been silent. Please you
To hear Cominius speak?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Most willingly;
But yet my caution was more pertinent
Than the rebuke you give it.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: He loves your people
But tie him not to be their bedfellow.
Worthy Cominius, speak.
Nay, keep your place.

units.speaker: First Senator
lines: Sit, Coriolanus; never shame to hear
What you have nobly done.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Your horror's pardon:
I had rather have my wounds to heal again
Than hear say how I got them.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Sir, I hope
My words disbench'd you not.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: No, sir: yet oft,
When blows have made me stay, I fled from words.
You soothed not, therefore hurt not: but
your people,
I love them as they weigh.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Pray now, sit down.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: I had rather have one scratch my head i' the sun
When the alarum were struck than idly sit
To hear my nothings monster'd.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Masters of the people,
Your multiplying spawn how can he flatter--
That's thousand to one good one--when you now see
He had rather venture all his limbs for honour
Than one on's ears to hear it? Proceed, Cominius.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: I shall lack voice: the deeds of Coriolanus
Should not be utter'd feebly. It is held
That valour is the chiefest virtue, and
Most dignifies the haver: if it be,
The man I speak of cannot in the world
Be singly counterpoised. At sixteen years,
When Tarquin made a head for Rome, he fought
Beyond the mark of others: our then dictator,
Whom with all praise I point at, saw him fight,
When with his Amazonian chin he drove
The bristled lips before him: be bestrid
An o'er-press'd Roman and i' the consul's view
Slew three opposers: Tarquin's self he met,
And struck him on his knee: in that day's feats,
When he might act the woman in the scene,
He proved best man i' the field, and for his meed
Was brow-bound with the oak. His pupil age
Man-enter'd thus, he waxed like a sea,
And in the brunt of seventeen battles since
He lurch'd all swords of the garland. For this last,
Before and in Corioli, let me say,
I cannot speak him home: he stopp'd the fliers;
And by his rare example made the coward
Turn terror into sport: as weeds before
A vessel under sail, so men obey'd
And fell below his stem: his sword, death's stamp,
Where it did mark, it took; from face to foot
He was a thing of blood, whose every motion
Was timed with dying cries: alone he enter'd
The mortal gate of the city, which he painted
With shunless destiny; aidless came off,
And with a sudden reinforcement struck
Corioli like a planet: now all's his:
When, by and by, the din of war gan pierce
His ready sense; then straight his doubled spirit
Re-quicken'd what in flesh was fatigate,
And to the battle came he; where he did
Run reeking o'er the lives of men, as if
'Twere a perpetual spoil: and till we call'd
Both field and city ours, he never stood
To ease his breast with panting.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Worthy man!

units.speaker: First Senator
lines: He cannot but with measure fit the honours
Which we devise him.

units.speaker: COMINIUS
lines: Our spoils he kick'd at,
And look'd upon things precious as they were
The common muck of the world: he covets less
Than misery itself would give; rewards
His deeds with doing them, and is content
To spend the time to end it.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: He's right noble:
Let him be call'd for.

units.speaker: First Senator
lines: Call Coriolanus.

units.speaker: Officer
lines: He doth appear.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: The senate, Coriolanus, are well pleased
To make thee consul.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: I do owe them still
My life and services.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: It then remains
That you do speak to the people.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: I do beseech you,
Let me o'erleap that custom, for I cannot
Put on the gown, stand naked and entreat them,
For my wounds' sake, to give their suffrage: please you
That I may pass this doing.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Sir, the people
Must have their voices; neither will they bate
One jot of ceremony.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Put them not to't:
Pray you, go fit you to the custom and
Take to you, as your predecessors have,
Your honour with your form.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: It is apart
That I shall blush in acting, and might well
Be taken from the people.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Mark you that?

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: To brag unto them, thus I did, and thus;
Show them the unaching scars which I should hide,
As if I had received them for the hire
Of their breath only!

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: Do not stand upon't.
We recommend to you, tribunes of the people,
Our purpose to them: and to our noble consul
Wish we all joy and honour.

units.speaker: Senators
lines: To Coriolanus come all joy and honour!

units.text: Flourish of cornets. Exeunt all but SICINIUS and BRUTUS

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: You see how he intends to use the people.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: May they perceive's intent! He will require them,
As if he did contemn what he requested
Should be in them to give.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Come, we'll inform them
Of our proceedings here: on the marketplace,
I know, they do attend us.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: Once, if he do require our voices, we ought not to deny him.

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: We may, sir, if we will.

units.speaker: Third Citizen
lines: We have power in ourselves to do it, but it is a
power that we have no power to do; for if he show us
his wounds and tell us his deeds, we are to put our
tongues into those wounds and speak for them; so, if
he tell us his noble deeds, we must also tell him
our noble acceptance of them. Ingratitude is
monstrous, and for the multitude to be ingrateful,
were to make a monster of the multitude: of the
which we being members, should bring ourselves to be
monstrous members.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: And to make us no better thought of, a little help
will serve; for once we stood up about the corn, he
himself stuck not to call us the many-headed multitude.

units.speaker: Third Citizen
lines: We have been called so of many; not that our heads
are some brown, some black, some auburn, some bald,
but that our wits are so diversely coloured: and
truly I think if all our wits were to issue out of
one skull, they would fly east, west, north, south,
and their consent of one direct way should be at
once to all the points o' the compass.

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: Think you so? Which way do you judge my wit would
fly?

units.speaker: Third Citizen
lines: Nay, your wit will not so soon out as another man's
will;'tis strongly wedged up in a block-head, but
if it were at liberty, 'twould, sure, southward.

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: Why that way?

units.speaker: Third Citizen
lines: To lose itself in a fog, where being three parts
melted away with rotten dews, the fourth would return
for conscience sake, to help to get thee a wife.

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: You are never without your tricks: you may, you may.

units.speaker: Third Citizen
lines: Are you all resolved to give your voices? But
that's no matter, the greater part carries it. I
say, if he would incline to the people, there was
never a worthier man.

units.text: Enter CORIOLANUS in a gown of humility, with MENENIUS

lines: Here he comes, and in the gown of humility: mark his
behavior. We are not to stay all together, but to
come by him where he stands, by ones, by twos, and
by threes. He's to make his requests by
particulars; wherein every one of us has a single
honour, in giving him our own voices with our own
tongues: therefore follow me, and I direct you how
you shall go by him.

units.speaker: All
lines: Content, content.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: O sir, you are not right: have you not known
The worthiest men have done't?

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: What must I say?
'I Pray, sir'--Plague upon't! I cannot bring
My tongue to such a pace:--'Look, sir, my wounds!
I got them in my country's service, when
Some certain of your brethren roar'd and ran
From the noise of our own drums.'

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: O me, the gods!
You must not speak of that: you must desire them
To think upon you.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Think upon me! hang 'em!
I would they would forget me, like the virtues
Which our divines lose by 'em.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: You'll mar all:
I'll leave you: pray you, speak to 'em, I pray you,
In wholesome manner.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Bid them wash their faces
And keep their teeth clean.
So, here comes a brace.
You know the cause, air, of my standing here.

units.speaker: Third Citizen
lines: We do, sir; tell us what hath brought you to't.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Mine own desert.

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: Your own desert!

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Ay, but not mine own desire.

units.speaker: Third Citizen
lines: How not your own desire?

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: No, sir,'twas never my desire yet to trouble the
poor with begging.

units.speaker: Third Citizen
lines: You must think, if we give you any thing, we hope to
gain by you.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Well then, I pray, your price o' the consulship?

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: The price is to ask it kindly.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Kindly! Sir, I pray, let me ha't: I have wounds to
show you, which shall be yours in private. Your
good voice, sir; what say you?

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: You shall ha' it, worthy sir.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: A match, sir. There's in all two worthy voices
begged. I have your alms: adieu.

units.speaker: Third Citizen
lines: But this is something odd.

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: An 'twere to give again,--but 'tis no matter.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Pray you now, if it may stand with the tune of your
voices that I may be consul, I have here the
customary gown.

units.speaker: Fourth Citizen
lines: You have deserved nobly of your country, and you
have not deserved nobly.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Your enigma?

units.speaker: Fourth Citizen
lines: You have been a scourge to her enemies, you have
been a rod to her friends; you have not indeed loved
the common people.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: You should account me the more virtuous that I have
not been common in my love. I will, sir, flatter my
sworn brother, the people, to earn a dearer
estimation of them; 'tis a condition they account
gentle: and since the wisdom of their choice is
rather to have my hat than my heart, I will practise
the insinuating nod and be off to them most
counterfeitly; that is, sir, I will counterfeit the
bewitchment of some popular man and give it
bountiful to the desirers. Therefore, beseech you,
I may be consul.

units.speaker: Fifth Citizen
lines: We hope to find you our friend; and therefore give
you our voices heartily.

units.speaker: Fourth Citizen
lines: You have received many wounds for your country.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: I will not seal your knowledge with showing them. I
will make much of your voices, and so trouble you no further.

units.speaker: Both Citizens
lines: The gods give you joy, sir, heartily!

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Most sweet voices!
Better it is to die, better to starve,
Than crave the hire which first we do deserve.
Why in this woolvish toge should I stand here,
To beg of Hob and Dick, that do appear,
Their needless vouches? Custom calls me to't:
What custom wills, in all things should we do't,
The dust on antique time would lie unswept,
And mountainous error be too highly heapt
For truth to o'er-peer. Rather than fool it so,
Let the high office and the honour go
To one that would do thus. I am half through;
The one part suffer'd, the other will I do.
Here come more voices.
Your voices: for your voices I have fought;
Watch'd for your voices; for Your voices bear
Of wounds two dozen odd; battles thrice six
I have seen and heard of; for your voices have
Done many things, some less, some more your voices:
Indeed I would be consul.

units.speaker: Sixth Citizen
lines: He has done nobly, and cannot go without any honest
man's voice.

units.speaker: Seventh Citizen
lines: Therefore let him be consul: the gods give him joy,
and make him good friend to the people!

units.speaker: All Citizens
lines: Amen, amen. God save thee, noble consul!

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Worthy voices!

units.text: Re-enter MENENIUS, with BRUTUS and SICINIUS

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: You have stood your limitation; and the tribunes
Endue you with the people's voice: remains
That, in the official marks invested, you
Anon do meet the senate.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Is this done?

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: The custom of request you have discharged:
The people do admit you, and are summon'd
To meet anon, upon your approbation.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: Where? at the senate-house?

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: There, Coriolanus.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: May I change these garments?

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: You may, sir.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
lines: That I'll straight do; and, knowing myself again,
Repair to the senate-house.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
lines: I'll keep you company. Will you along?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: We stay here for the people.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Fare you well.
He has it now, and by his looks methink
'Tis warm at 's heart.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: With a proud heart he wore his humble weeds.
will you dismiss the people?

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: How now, my masters! have you chose this man?

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: He has our voices, sir.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: We pray the gods he may deserve your loves.

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: Amen, sir: to my poor unworthy notice,
He mock'd us when he begg'd our voices.

units.speaker: Third Citizen
lines: Certainly
He flouted us downright.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: No,'tis his kind of speech: he did not mock us.

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: Not one amongst us, save yourself, but says
He used us scornfully: he should have show'd us
His marks of merit, wounds received for's country.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Why, so he did, I am sure.

units.speaker: Citizens
lines: No, no; no man saw 'em.

units.speaker: Third Citizen
lines: He said he had wounds, which he could show
in private;
And with his hat, thus waving it in scorn,
'I would be consul,' says he: 'aged custom,
But by your voices, will not so permit me;
Your voices therefore.' When we granted that,
Here was 'I thank you for your voices: thank you:
Your most sweet voices: now you have left
your voices,
I have no further with you.' Was not this mockery?

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Why either were you ignorant to see't,
Or, seeing it, of such childish friendliness
To yield your voices?

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Could you not have told him
As you were lesson'd, when he had no power,
But was a petty servant to the state,
He was your enemy, ever spake against
Your liberties and the charters that you bear
I' the body of the weal; and now, arriving
A place of potency and sway o' the state,
If he should still malignantly remain
Fast foe to the plebeii, your voices might
Be curses to yourselves? You should have said
That as his worthy deeds did claim no less
Than what he stood for, so his gracious nature
Would think upon you for your voices and
Translate his malice towards you into love,
Standing your friendly lord.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Thus to have said,
As you were fore-advised, had touch'd his spirit
And tried his inclination; from him pluck'd
Either his gracious promise, which you might,
As cause had call'd you up, have held him to
Or else it would have gall'd his surly nature,
Which easily endures not article
Tying him to aught; so putting him to rage,
You should have ta'en the advantage of his choler
And pass'd him unelected.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Did you perceive
He did solicit you in free contempt
When he did need your loves, and do you think
That his contempt shall not be bruising to you,
When he hath power to crush? Why, had your bodies
No heart among you? or had you tongues to cry
Against the rectorship of judgment?

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Have you
Ere now denied the asker? and now again
Of him that did not ask, but mock, bestow
Your sued-for tongues?

units.speaker: Third Citizen
lines: He's not confirm'd; we may deny him yet.

units.speaker: Second Citizen
lines: And will deny him:
I'll have five hundred voices of that sound.

units.speaker: First Citizen
lines: I twice five hundred and their friends to piece 'em.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Get you hence instantly, and tell those friends,
They have chose a consul that will from them take
Their liberties; make them of no more voice
Than dogs that are as often beat for barking
As therefore kept to do so.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Let them assemble,
And on a safer judgment all revoke
Your ignorant election; enforce his pride,
And his old hate unto you; besides, forget not
With what contempt he wore the humble weed,
How in his suit he scorn'd you; but your loves,
Thinking upon his services, took from you
The apprehension of his present portance,
Which most gibingly, ungravely, he did fashion
After the inveterate hate he bears you.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Lay
A fault on us, your tribunes; that we laboured,
No impediment between, but that you must
Cast your election on him.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: Say, you chose him
More after our commandment than as guided
By your own true affections, and that your minds,
Preoccupied with what you rather must do
Than what you should, made you against the grain
To voice him consul: lay the fault on us.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Ay, spare us not. Say we read lectures to you.
How youngly he began to serve his country,
How long continued, and what stock he springs of,
The noble house o' the Marcians, from whence came
That Ancus Marcius, Numa's daughter's son,
Who, after great Hostilius, here was king;
Of the same house Publius and Quintus were,
That our beat water brought by conduits hither;
And  [Censorinus,]  nobly named so,
Twice being  [by the people chosen]  censor,
Was his great ancestor.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: One thus descended,
That hath beside well in his person wrought
To be set high in place, we did commend
To your remembrances: but you have found,
Scaling his present bearing with his past,
That he's your fixed enemy, and revoke
Your sudden approbation.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Say, you ne'er had done't--
Harp on that still--but by our putting on;
And presently, when you have drawn your number,
Repair to the Capitol.

units.speaker: All
lines: We will so: almost all
Repent in their election.

units.speaker: BRUTUS
lines: Let them go on;
This mutiny were better put in hazard,
Than stay, past doubt, for greater:
If, as his nature is, he fall in rage
With their refusal, both observe and answer
The vantage of his anger.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
lines: To the Capitol, come:
We will be there before the stream o' the people;
And this shall seem, as partly 'tis, their own,
Which we have goaded onward.

units.text: Cornets. Enter CORIOLANUS, MENENIUS, all the Gentry, COMINIUS, TITUS LARTIUS, and other Senators

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
